paragraphs.body: A Fork in the Road Excerpt Two
Chapter Seven

They hit slight traffic, just as Julie’s dad had predicted. The highway was crowded with drivers and families trying to reach their destinations. Cars passed by slowly in the neighboring lanes, speeding up before realizing that there was a car in front of them and slowing as they neared it. A rusty red Jaguar tried cutting into the middle lane. Julie’s dad honked in indignation.
“What the hell are you doing?” he yelled at the car. The Jaguar retreated back into the left lane, as if it could hear him.
“Jeff, stop, he’s fine now,” Julie’s mom gestured to the car, which was now grunting and sputtering across from them.
Julie’s dad relented and relaxed back into the driver’s seat.
“Music?” he asked, and without waiting for an answer, Julie’s dad turned on the radio.

annotations.comment: I really like your use of scene in this section (although I could use a note helping me know where this is compared to what I’ve already read). “Julie’s dad” and “Julie’s mom” are going to get awkward. Just “Mom” might work better for you.

paragraphs.body: The piano intro to “Christmas Lights” played through the speakers, the lower notes booming through the backseat speakers.
“Dad, no, skip it, this song is sad,” Nora insisted.

annotations.comment: Nora is Julie’s sister? So this would be her dad too? Awkward!

paragraphs.body: “No, keep it,” Julie’s mom said. She reached over and turned up the volume.
Julie’s dad shrugged and continued driving.

Julie slumped in her seat as the song played. Suddenly, she was no longer in her family’s car on the way to Christmas dinner, but in Luca’s Honda Accord, that twenty-five-year-old, barely-hanging-on, shaking-beneath-your-feet Accord, with its busted speakers and dusty dashboard and everything else Julie did not want to picture from the day of the breakup.

annotations.comment: Nice. It’s a nice touch that all of the anger and traffic didn’t upset her but a song did.

paragraphs.body: Her mind wandered to the Christmas before in her apartment, decorating the miniature Christmas tree and sprucing up the living room for the holiday season.

annotations.comment: Last year?

paragraphs.body: “Remind me again why we have to set up everything immediately after Thanksgiving ends,” Luca said. He was crouched on the carpeted floor trying to find the closest outlet to plug the tree lights into. “You still have leftover turkey and stuffing in your fridge.”

annotations.comment: I’d use “Luca had said” here to help us make the transition and then use past tense verbs (as you have) for the rest of the flashback.

paragraphs.body: “It’s November 28th,” Julie jokingly scoffed. She added the last ornament to the tree, making sure not to get any pine needles on the ground as she gingerly placed it on the branch.

annotations.comment: Just scoffed.

paragraphs.body: “And?” Luca pressed.
Julie smiled. She knew he was trying to get a rise out of her. “And it means Thanksgiving's over, which means it’s almost December 1st, which means the Christmas season is basically here.”
“Well, not yet,” Luca said, teasing. He plugged the lights in. The tree glowed and twinkled at them, the lights creating a warm, yellowed shadow against the blank wall.
“Well, almost,” Julie teased back.
Luca backed away from the outlet, marveling at the newly adorned tree. He leaned over and kissed her forehead.
“Fine, almost,” he said. He put his arm around her as Julie leaned into his shoulder.

annotations.comment: The dialogue is believable and realistic — but mostly just establishes that they have a nice relationship. What else can we learn from this?
annotations.context_right: said. He put his arm around her as Julie leaned into his shoulder.

paragraphs.body: The Bluetooth speaker sat perched across from them on the coffee table. The instrumental version of “Sleigh Bells” started playing.
“We’ve heard this one already,” Julie said as she broke away from Luca’s arms. She walked over to her phone and skipped to the next song.
“And this song is any better?” Luca said as Chris Martin began singing. “Julie, this song is so sad.”
“I don’t care,” Julie said. She plopped herself down next to Luca and curled up next to him again. Luca rested his chin on her head. “I love this song.”
“It’s still sad,” Luca protested.
Julie shook her head. “Shhh…It’s beautiful,” she said, “just listen to the lyrics for a second.”

annotations.comment: I wondered if it would make more sense for Julie to ask her dad to skip the song in the car — or if Nora knows what this song means to her sister?

paragraphs.body: And they sat, on the rough carpeted floor beside the Christmas tree, and listened as the chorus crescendoed into the bridge, the sounds of the drums and the piano keys muffled through Julie’s old speaker. It was the perfect Christmas scene: the small tree twinkling in the corner, stockings for Julie and her three roommates hanging precariously by a single pin above the fireplace, and the music to complement it all. Outside, strong gusts of winter wind blew past the apartment. They shook the green and red fairy lights dangling from the porch banister. If Julie looked close enough, she swore she could see snowflakes dancing on the winter breeze. But she told herself it was wishful thinking.

The song ended, and Julie and Luca remained on the floor, closely nestled in each other’s arms. Another variation of “Sleigh Bells” played through the speakers, but this time, Julie ignored it. She re-adjusted her head, her temple pressed into the fabrics of Luca’s sweater. Luca shifted slightly before repositioning his arm around Julie. His chin still leaning softly against her head, Julie could feel his lips tug into a smile. Julie smiled in return.

annotations.comment: Again, it’s nice! But why do we need to see this scene? Is present day Julie thinking anything about it — does she have any opinions now?

paragraphs.body: All the world was quiet for a moment. There was nowhere else to go. There were no errands to run, no homework to finish, no other obligations that demanded their attention. It was just Julie and Luca, in the little living room, in that little apartment, tucked cozily away in the late November weather.
“Julie?” Luca said.
“Mmm?” was all Julie replied.
“I love you,” Luca said.
Julie smiled again and wrapped both of his arms around her. “I love you too,” she said.

annotations.comment: Pointing one more time to the potential this scene has to be *devastating* to the Julie in the car today.

paragraphs.body: “I fucking hate this song,” Nora said as “Dominic the Donkey” began playing.
“Language!” Julie’s dad said. He turned down the music and switched to another radio station playing Christmas music. Everyone calmed down at the sound of “Jingle Bell Rock.”

annotations.comment: I would perhaps like to see them not calm.

paragraphs.body: Eventually, the traffic died down, and the family car breezed down long, wide country roads. They passed tall, skeletal trees adorned in a freshly fallen frost and houses decked out in Christmas cheer. Some were more moderate in their Christmas spirit and others had their lawns completely covered in Christmas cheer, with light-up, inflatable Santa’s and reindeer statues clouding every inch of the grass.
“Oh my God, Julie, look at that one,” Nora said, gawking at an inflatable dinosaur in a Santa hat.
Julie turned towards the other side of the car and shrugged.
“Yeah,” she tried to say convincingly, “that’s pretty bad.”
Nora, noticing Julie’s unenthusiasm, reached for Julie’s hand. Julie took it.
“It’s gonna be okay,” Nora reassured her, “no one’s gonna know.”

annotations.comment: I would love for this moment to arrive earlier — make it part of the arguing in the car.

paragraphs.body: Julie nodded as if trying to convince herself that that was, in fact, the case. “They’ll know,” Julie said. She patted Nora’s hand.

After more inflatable Santa’s, reindeer, and overdone lawns, Julie’s dad made a left past a mailbox marked 39 in faded, gold lettering and pulled into the narrow driveway, which was already littered with cars and a bright, red truck parked near the shed.

annotations.comment: Plurals don’t need an apostrophe.

paragraphs.body: Julie gathered up the large bag of presents, and she and her family made their way to the front of the house.

annotations.comment: You have written beautifully and conveyed the trip well at a nice pace. But why did we need to see this scene? How has the plot moved forward?

paragraphs.body: Chapter Eight
The sights and sounds of the holidays greeted Julie at the door as her grandmother brought her into a hug.

“It’s so good to see you, honey!” Julie’s grandmother exclaimed. She broke away from the hug and admired Julie from afar, her hands clutching Julie’s arms as she eyed her from head to toe. “You look fantastic!”
Julie breathed a sigh of relief. So maybe she wouldn’t be bombarded with questions. Maybe this would be a normal Christmas dinner afterall. No Luca. No questions. No worries.

annotations.comment: This might be clear in the sections I haven’t read, but does anyone in the family besides Nora know that Luca and Julie broke up? The rest of them seem oblivious.

paragraphs.body: “Thanks, Grandma,” Julie said. She could feel the weight on her shoulders lifting rapidly as she crossed the threshold into the house. She could feel the animal declawing itself from Julie’s turtleneck, her breathing becoming more full, steadier. Nora was right. How could she not have seen it before? Julie's grandmother didn’t know. The family didn’t know. And they weren’t going to know. And it was Christmas! Surely the spirit of Christmas was enough to distract them, all of them.
“So,” Julie’s grandmother started, leading Julie, Nora, and their parents through the foyer, “where’s the boyfriend?”

annotations.comment: Ah ha! Julie’s grandmother (who is also Nora’s grandmother) needs a name.

paragraphs.body: Her grandmother’s tone was teasing, playful, but Julie couldn’t shake the question. She froze in place. The weights returned to her shoulders, and the animal slowly but surely snaked its way up to her chest, welcoming itself back to its home on her chest.
Julie stammered. “Well… um –”
“Mom,” Julie’s mom interjected over Julie’s shoulder. Julie turned to see her mom making wild gestures across her throat. “Not right now.”
“Oh,” Julie’s grandmother said. Her eyes darted rapidly between Julie and her mother. “Okay,” she said in a loud whisper, more to Julie than to Julie’s mother.

annotations.comment: A nice moment.

paragraphs.body: She led them into the living room where the family was gathered, clustered among the couches and tables of hors d’oeuvres.

Just like every year, Julie’s grandmother’s house perfectly emulated the Christmas spirit. The entire mantel was lined with miniature nutcrackers, each with a different colored uniform, their teeth broken and battered from years of Julie and her cousins playing with them on the living room floor. A shimmering silver “Christmas” sign sat proudly in the middle of the mantel, overlooking the stockings and the fireplace crackling and cackling with life. The piano donned a Christmas scene of its very own. Wads of white cotton snow covered every inch of the piano lid, dotted with village figurines caroling amongst the houses in the village, their music clutched in hand and their frilled bonnets fastened neatly to their heads.

annotations.comment: You really like to set scenes! :)

paragraphs.body: No sooner had Julie taken in the entirety of the living room than she was ambushed by her Aunt Helen, her thick, knee-length fur coat nearly knocking Julie over.

annotations.comment: Indoors? I’m enjoying the fact that Julie’s hope that no one would notice Luca’s absence is absolutely not happening. You could have some fun earlier with either Nora convincing Julie that she is right or wrong.

paragraphs.body: “Hello, sweetheart!” Aunt Helen exclaimed merrily. She swept Julie into a hug. As she pulled away, her eyes looked to Julie’s left, as if noting the empty space.
“Where’s Luca?” she asked.

Julie froze again. The emptiness lingered at her side. She wanted nothing more than to lean back into it, only instead of leaning into thin air, she was leaning back onto Luca’s chest, the fibers of his snowman Christmas sweater tickling her hair.

“He’s um…” Julie stammered, trying to collect herself quicker than she had a few minutes ago. She half-expected her mother to swoop in and save her, but her mother was deep in conversation on the other side of the room. Julie began playing with her fingers, a nervous habit she had been trying to break, but she was in too deep now. Now was not the time to break it.

“He’s back in Maine with his family,” Julie blurted. Her voice was trembling, desperate, as if questioning the validity of her own statement. Her thumbs circled like hawks around one another, her nails digging into her neighboring thumb as she toyed with her fingers.
“Oh, that’s nice,” Aunt Helen said, beaming. Julie breathed an inconspicuous sigh of relief. “Well, tell him that he will be missed.”

annotations.comment: Love that Aunt Helen is oblivious.

paragraphs.body: “I will,” Julie nodded.
“And tell him we’ll see him at your graduation party,” Aunt Helen added.
“Will do,” Julie said, her voice a little hesitant. Aunt Helen didn’t seem to notice. She turned away as another one of their relatives grabbed Aunt Helen’s attention.
Julie started to make her ways towards the charcuterie board when great Aunt Carol stepped in front of her, blocking her path.

annotations.comment: Let us see this rest on Julie. She got what she wanted — supposedly — by not telling her family about the break up. So, how does she feel now?

paragraphs.body: “Hi, honey!” Aunt Carol exclaimed, practically mirroring Aunt Helen’s enthusiasm. She gave Julie a slobbery peck on the cheek.
“Hi, Aunt Carol,” Julie said. Julie looked over Aunt Carol’s shoulder. She watched in envy as Nora smeared a slice of brie and jam onto a rosemary cracker.
“So how are you? How’s college?” she said, staring at Julie as if she couldn’t believe that Julie was actually a grown-up.
“College is good, everything’s good,” Julie said. “Just getting ready to graduate.”
“I know, your mother was telling me,” Aunt Carol nodded in satisfaction, “one more semester to go, huh?”
“Yep, just one,” Julie said.
“How exciting!” Aunt Carol clapped her hands together. “You must be thrilled.”
“Yeah,” Julie said, nodding in agreement, “I’m pretty excited.”
“And you and Luca are going to Boston, right?” Aunt Carol asked, “for grad school?”
“Oh, um… well-” Julie said, stumbling.

annotations.comment: Consider how these conversations can show us Julie’s conflict. These women seem perfectly pleasant and interchangeable. What happens if one is more aggressive, or admits she’s never liked Luca, or in some way gets under Julie’s?sin?

paragraphs.body: “Boston’s a great city!” Aunt Carol interjected. “So much to do. I actually did my undergrad there, at Boston University. But that was a long time ago, back when your mother was still in diapers.” She waved her hand as if she couldn’t believe how much time had passed.

“You two will love it there,” she continued. “So much to see. And so many people! I have a friend, her son has lived in Boston for ten years… loves it! He and his wife met getting their Master’s, and they’ve lived there ever since. I can contact him if you want, maybe he can help you get situated, find an apartment…”

Julie felt her eyes watering, but she bit her lip to stop it before her vision blurred. She listened as Aunt Carol went on and on about Boston, about her friend’s thirty-year-old son, about him and his wife, their townhouse, their dog, Fergie, all their favorite restaurants downtown.

annotations.text: her lip to stop it before her vision
annotations.comment: A great opportunity for her to lose it.

paragraphs.body: “And I can give you his number if you want,” Aunt Carol said. “His name’s Mark, he would love to talk to you. He helped my daughter with her BU application.”
“That would be wonderful, Aunt Carol, thank you,” Julie started. She summoned up the rest of her courage. There was no point in avoiding it now.
“But I’ve decided not to go to Boston.”

annotations.comment: Great. I would like to see her get here with the first aunt and sooner.

paragraphs.body: “Oh,” Aunt Carol said, clearly a bit caught off guard, “you two are staying in Burlington, then?”
“Luca’s going,” Julie said, realizing she was separating herself, her future, from Luca. Aloud. For the first time. “But I’m not.”

annotations.comment: Excellent.

paragraphs.body: “Oh, I see,” Aunt Carol repeated. She nodded uncertainty this time. It was clear she was still a little confused. “Did you and Luca…” Aunt Carol let the unfinished sentence hang in the air.

annotations.comment: I would imagine Aunt Carol feels a little annoyed, having been allowed to go on and on about something that’s not happening.

paragraphs.body: “We just broke up,”Julie said. She prayed no one else heard her reveal this news.
“Oh,” Aunt Carol said. Julie wished there was another word for “oh” in Aunt Carol’s vocabulary.
There was a slight pause. Aunt Carol spoke.
“When did you guys…” her question drifted off again.

annotations.comment: I wondered if there’s another question Aunt Carol might ask here, which might get to Julie a bit more. This is repeating information we know.

paragraphs.body: “A few days ago,” Julie replied. She tried maintaining a flat tone, but her voice shook at the end of her sentence.
“Aww, honey,”Aunt Carol said, “I’m so sorry.”
“It’s okay,” Julie replied, even though it wasn’t okay at all.
Aunt Carol brought her cardigan more tightly to her. “So, what are your plans after-”

“Julie!” Nora called over the sea of relatives. Julie whipped her head around to face her sister, who was now carrying a paper plate packed with cheese, crackers, mini meatballs, and stuffed mushrooms – a fan favorite!
“Hey,” Julie said. She hugged Nora, doing her best not to crush the assorted goodies.

annotations.comment: Without knowing them better, I don’t know if this is weird or not.

paragraphs.body: “Hey, Aunt Carol,” Nora said.
“Hi, sweetie,” Aunt Carol replied, leaning in to give Nora a peck on the cheek.
“Jules, Mom wants you for a sec,” Nora said, turning to Julie.
Julie just realized she had a small tear on her under eye. She wiped it away.
“You better go see what your mother wants,” Aunt Carol patted Julie’s shoulder.

annotations.comment: I love the idea that everyone wants this conversation to end.

paragraphs.body: “Definitely,” Julie nodded in agreement. “It was great to see you, Aunt Carol.”
“It was great to see you too, Julie,” Aunt Carol said, beaming again. “And you too, Nora.”
The girls turned away and walked to the opposite side of the living room.
“Thanks,” Julie said.
“Anytime,” Nora said through a mouthful of meatball. “That woman just talks and talks.”
“Mmm.”
“Does she know?” Nora asked.
“She knows,” Julie said, sighing.

annotations.comment: Make this more of a moment for Julie — she’s finally told someone besides her immediate family.

paragraphs.body: “Hors d'œuvre?” Nora held the plate in front of Julie.
“Yes please,” Julie said hungrily. She grabbed a stuffed mushroom and popped it into her mouth.
Chapter Nine

Dinner went by as well as it could have. Just like every year, Julie’s grandmother entered the living room, her forehead coated in sweat from the array of pots and pans heating and boiling over the stove, announcing that dinner was almost ready.

And, at her quiet, graceful entrance into the living room, Julie, Nora, and a handful of the cousins made their way towards the kitchen, zig zagging around the spread of easy chairs and snack tables and their grandmother’s bumbling thirteen-year-old dog, Freddy.
As Julie gathered the special occasion plates and assembled them gingerly into piles on the kitchen island, her grandmother snuck up behind her, her apron grazing the neighboring cabinets.
“So,” Julie’s grandmother said as she adjusted the temperature on one of the burners, “you and Luca are…” She trailed off before she could finish her sentence, like she was holding herself back.

annotations.comment: I wondered about the timing here. She’s about to serve an enormous family dinner. Is this the moment she brings the break up up?

paragraphs.body: “Yeah,” Julie said. She set down a small pile of holiday-themed napkins on the counter. “How do you know?”
“Your mom told me,” Julie’s grandmother replied, nodding sadly.
Her voice was hushed, almost at a whisper, but no sooner had Julie’s grandmother uttered those words than Julie’s older cousin, Josephine, lept into the conversation.
“Told you what?” she asked, scooping large spoonfuls of mashed potatoes onto a porcelain tray.
A hush fell between Julie and her grandmother’s already awkward conversation.

Julie looked behind to her grandmother, then to Josephine. “Oh, well… Luca and I…” she started, stumbling on her words for what felt like the hundredth time today. “We broke up,” she finished, more smoothly this time.

annotations.comment: The action here feels a little repetitive — we’re watching poor Julie have to do this sucky thing but seeing it over and over actually lessens its impact. I would consider showing us just one scene of her telling and then summarizing the rest (the exception would be if someone here reacts badly).

paragraphs.body: “Aww Jules, that’s terrible, I’m so sorry,” Josephine said. She gingerly placed the tray on the counter, her arms outstretched for a hug. Julie reluctantly went in for the hug.

“Wait, what happened?” a voice called over from the opposite side of the kitchen island. Josephine quickly broke the hug, and she and Julie turned to face Maverick, another one of Julie’s older cousins.

“Julie and Luca broke up,” Josephine blurted before Julie could get a word in. At a loss for what to do, Julie simply nodded in agreement as Ella squeezed her hand in support – a little too tightly for Julie’s taste.

annotations.comment: Unless we’re going to see Josephine and Maverick again, we don’t need to actually watch them get this news.

paragraphs.body: “What?”
Note: These pages are from pages 22-32 of my novel.

annotations.comment: As always, you write beautifully, Luna. Your sentence construction and dialogue are gorgeous, and I love how you build a setting so well. Here, again, my biggest question is what it is that Julie wants? I see her sort of going through the motions of how this might realistically play out, but without much change or surprise. She doesn’t even fight to not tell anyone for long. What happens to this scene if you choose to have Julie be PISSED OFF about the break up, or almost overwhelmingly sad? Imagine if her whole family has convinced her to just, like, smile through it and then the minute Aunt Carol asks about Luca, it all goes to hell? Or perhaps you want a different course for Julie, which of course! It’s your story. But let her WANT something and try to get it. I’ve enjoyed working with you Amelia! Good luck on your 50 pages!
